anchor.snippet: mathematical sequence of non-zero numbers where each term after the first is found by multiplying the previous one by a fixed number
the sequence 2, 6, 18, 54, ... is a geometric progression with a common ratio of 3
10, 5, 2.5, 1.25, ... is a geometric sequence with a common ratio of 1/2
Examples of a geometric sequence are powers
The sum of a geometric progression's terms is called a geometric series
the terms in a geometric series are also said to be in continued proportion
The n th term of a geometric sequence with initial value
Geometric sequences satisfy the linear recurrence relation
Geometric sequences also satisfy the nonlinear recurrence relation
When the common ratio of a geometric sequence is positive, the sequence's terms will all share the sign of the first term
the sequence's terms alternate between positive and negative; this is called an alternating sequence
the sequence 1, −3, 9, −27, 81, −243, ... is an alternating geometric sequence
When the initial term and common ratio are complex numbers, the terms' complex arguments follow an arithmetic progression
If the absolute value of the common ratio is smaller than 1, the terms will decrease in magnitude and approach zero
If the absolute value of the common ratio is greater than 1, the terms will increase in magnitude and approach infinity
If the absolute value of the common ratio equals 1, the terms will stay the same size indefinitely
exponentiating each term of an arithmetic progression yields a geometric progression, while taking the logarithm of each term in a geometric progression yields an arithmetic progression
A geometric series is a series derived from the number of terms
An infinite geometric series is convergent if the absolute value of a common ratio is less than one
an infinite geometric series is divergent if if the absolute value of a common ratio is greater than one
An infinite geometric series when
The partial product of a geometric progression up to the term with power
this is equivalent to taking the geometric mean of the partial progression's first and last individual terms and then raising that mean to the power given by the number of terms
the sum of an arithmetic sequence is the number of terms times the arithmetic mean of the first and last individual terms
The exponent of r is the sum of an arithmetic sequence
